chapter_summary: Chapter 7 presents France's 1989 educational reforms as a tragic 'natural experiment' that replicated the American educational decline by replacing a communal national curriculum with individualized, skill-based pedagogy. Hirsch argues that the subsequent collapse of French student achievement and equity serves as definitive proof that 'providential individualism' is the primary cause of educational failure in the modern West.

claims.text: The educational decline in French elementary schools following 1989 constitutes a 'débâcle' equivalent in gravity to France's 1940 military defeat.
claims.source_passage: To speak of “The Educational Fall of France” seems at first rhetorical overkill—equating mere lower scores in French elementary schools with the military defeat of France in World War II. But it isn’t just an over-the-top play on words. It was the explicit theme of an important 2007 book edited by Laurent Lafforgue and Liliane Lurçat: La Débâcle de l’École: Une Tragédie Incomprise.

claims.text: The decline of the French school system was caused by avoidable intellectual mistakes made by top intellectuals in 1989.
claims.source_passage: His view, which I agree with, is that top French intellectuals made big avoidable mistakes in 1989, just as higher-ups had made serious, avoidable military mistakes in 1940.

claims.text: France's adoption of skill-based, individualized educational principles caused it to decline from one of the best and most equitable systems to one of the worst and least equitable.
claims.source_passage: The data showed another educational system that, like our own, had adopted skill-based, individualized, educational principles, and subsequently declined from being among the best and most equitable large school systems in the world to being one of the worst and least equitable.

claims.text: The educational decline in France is a fully documented repetition of the decline experienced in the United States.
claims.source_passage: The data showed that France had experienced a fully documented repetition of the US decline. Ours began showing up in the 1960s. The origins of our decline can be clearly traced to our having made similar intellectual mistakes half a century before.

claims.text: The massive decline in American verbal test scores between 1960 and 1980 was caused by curriculum reforms rooted in progressive ideas from the 1930s and 1940s.
claims.source_passage: If we insist that the nationwide progressive “reforms” of the curriculum in the 1930s and 1940s did not chiefly cause that disaster, we simply protect the faulty ideas that are the key to the whole conundrum.

claims.text: The 1960-1980 US test score decline affected all students, regardless of socioeconomic status.
claims.source_passage: Scholars have now shown that there was a significant score drop on virtually all US school tests—not just on the SAT—by all students, rich and poor, during the period 1965–1980.

claims.text: Demographic change can be eliminated as a cause for the decline in test scores in Iowa and Minnesota because their pupil demographics remained stable during the decline.
claims.source_passage: administered to virtually all high school juniors and seniors in those states at a time when their pupil demographics were stable—thus eliminating demographic change as a cause of the declines in those states.

claims.text: The idea-system of 'providential individualism' acts like a virus that prevents educational improvement across national boundaries.
claims.source_passage: Like a virus, providential individualism knows no national boundary or political party. As I will indicate in this chapter, the virus has also infected Britain and Sweden, and until recently Germany.

claims.text: American NAEP verbal scores for seventeen-year-olds have remained flat since 1971 because the national decline was already well under way when sampling began.
claims.source_passage: According to the National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP) the verbal scores of our seventeen-year-olds have stayed flat ever since 1971, the year we first began national sampling, a decade after our decline was well under way.

claims.text: The French experience provides scientific proof of the cause and cure for the 'educational disease' affecting New York and the US.
claims.source_passage: The cause of the disease has now been definitively determined in Paris—and the cure as well. We should not hesitate to import that knowledge.

claims.text: The cause and cure for the American educational 'illness' have been definitively determined by recent educational outcomes in Paris.
claims.source_passage: The cause of the disease has now been definitively determined in Paris—and the cure as well.

claims.text: Germany served as the nineteenth-century nursery of progressive education.
claims.source_passage: Germany, which was the nineteenth-century nursery of progressive education, and has had a decisive influence on educational thought and policy in the United States.

claims.text: Recent French educational data constitutes a natural experiment regarding the effects of educational theory on ten-year-old students.
claims.source_passage: France, with its excellent record keeping, has in effect recently completed an unintended natural experiment with ten-year-old students over a period of twenty years.

claims.text: French statistical and sociological analysis of education is equal in quality to the American National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP).
claims.source_passage: The statistical and sociological analysis is on a par with our research gold standard—the National Assessment of Educational Progress.

claims.text: American ten-year-olds are not fundamentally different from French ten-year-olds regarding cognitive proficiency in math and reading.
claims.source_passage: We are not so imbued with American exceptionalism as to believe that American ten-year-olds are fundamentally different from French ones with respect to math and reading proficiency.

claims.text: In the 1980s, France and Sweden possessed the best large school systems in Europe in terms of achievement and equity.
claims.source_passage: In the 1980s, France could celebrate (with Sweden) the best large school systems in Europe.

claims.text: The pre-1989 French national curriculum was effective because it allocated specific time to specific topics within each discipline and grade level.
claims.source_passage: The document allocated a specific number of minutes per week for the study of topics in each discipline in each grade. The topics in history, math, science, civics, literature, and music were outlined with some specificity.

claims.text: When a state controls basic educational content, publishers gain competitive advantage by improving the effectiveness and presentation of materials rather than controlling the content.
claims.source_passage: The publishers had no control over the basic content of schooling; they gained their competitive advantage by presenting the content attractively and effectively at a good price.

claims.text: France's earlier success in overcoming inequality was due to universal preschool attendance and a communal primary school with a common curriculum.
claims.source_passage: I had long attributed this earlier French accomplishment in overcoming inequality to the excellent French preschool system being attended by nearly 100 percent of four- and five-year- olds, and also to the fact that after preschool, children entered a highly communal primary school with a well-developed common curriculum.

claims.text: The loi Jospin of 1989 was a catastrophic educational policy change for France.
claims.source_passage: the intellectual preparation for the catastrophic loi Jospin of 1989 had been brewing in French education schools since the 1960s.

claims.text: The 1989 French reform was characterized by a paradoxical process of 'wrenching, centralized' decentralization.
claims.source_passage: Because French schooling was centrally controlled, it decentralized itself in 1989 in a wrenching, centralized manner.

claims.text: The sudden and universal nature of France's 1989 curricular changes allows for confident causal inferences that are impossible in the decentralized United States system.
claims.source_passage: The suddenness and universality of its curricular changes enable us now to make some confident inferences of the sort that are impossible in the United States, where specific curricular content over time is largely unspecified and unknown.

claims.text: The common curriculum of the French primary school was the foundational theme of French education from the 18th century until the 1980s.
claims.source_passage: the common curriculum of the French primary school was a recurrent theme of French education from Condorcet in 1790 to President Giscard d’Estaing in 1977

claims.text: The traditional aim of French schooling from the 18th century until 1977 was to provide all children the same knowledge to unify society and reduce inequality.
claims.source_passage: the common curriculum of the French primary school was a recurrent theme of French education from Condorcet in 1790 to President Giscard d’Estaing in 1977, who stated the basic idea of French schooling: to enable all French children to acquire the same knowledge in order to unify society and reduce inequality.

claims.text: The 1989 French educational reform overthrew the tradition of a common culture in favor of respecting individual and local home cultures.
claims.source_passage: In 1989, France completely overthrew that tradition by passing a law saying in effect that each child would go to a different primary school and middle school. Instead of acquiring the same culture, each school was to respect and develop the child’s home culture and conform to the individual characteristics of each locality and to the individual characteristics of each child.

claims.text: The 1989 Jospin law was structurally based on the Bourdieu-Gros Report, which critiqued the national curriculum as an exclusion of non-elite cultures.
claims.source_passage: The structure of the radical change was to be based on a new report called the Bourdieu-Gros Report. Pierre Bourdieu had been chosen to head the committee (which also included Jacques Derrida) because he had been in the forefront of scholars who complained that the old-fashioned national curriculum was not as egalitarian as it claimed to be.

claims.text: The 1989 reform decentralized French education by creating autonomous school districts and allowing individual schools to set their own unique aims and curricula.
claims.source_passage: the school itself was to have a measure of autonomy, with each school required to set forth its own special aims—making its plan appropriate to local values.

claims.text: The Jospin law utilized progressive theories regarding 'natural rhythms of development' to organize school cycles.
claims.source_passage: a more natural course of learning divided into cycles designed to coincide with the natural rhythms of development and growth.

claims.text: The creation of twenty-eight new education colleges in 1989 served to institutionalize and codify progressive pedagogical theory in France.
claims.source_passage: The Jospin law also included the institution of twenty-eight new education colleges... They were to codify the profession of pedagogy in a scientific and orderly way (translation: they would teach progressive theory).

claims.text: The national standards produced under the 1989 law (the 'socle') were too vague and nonspecific to effectively guide instruction.
claims.source_passage: Now known as “the platform” (socle), the standards document was astonishingly short and nonspecific—nonexistent in practical terms.

claims.text: The 1989 reform intentionally shifted educational focus from the acquisition of facts (encyclopedism) to the development of all-purpose skills.
claims.source_passage: The emphasis was to be on skills, not facts, based on the statement in the Bourdieu-Gros Report that all-purpose skills are far more important than the piling up of knowledge.

claims.text: The preamble of the 1989 law codified a child-centered approach where students are responsible for elaborating their own curricula.
claims.source_passage: The preamble of the new 1989 law made clear that French education was not going to be “lockstep” any longer. It was going to be child-centered... Pupils and students will elaborate their own curricula according to their own aspirations and capacities.

claims.text: The French Ministry of Education continues to instruct schools to prioritize the plurality and diversity of student aptitudes despite years of educational decline.
claims.source_passage: The schools were commanded to adopt individualism by taking “into account the plurality and diversity of aptitudes of each pupil” (an instruction the French Ministry of Education still gives to schools today, even after many years of decline).

claims.text: The 1989 Bourdieu-Gros Report asserted that all-purpose skills are more important than the accumulation of knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Gros Report that all-purpose skills are far more important than the piling up of knowledge.

claims.text: The 1989 French education law mandated that education be child-centered and organized for the benefit of the student rather than being 'lockstep.'
claims.source_passage: The very preamble of the new 1989 law made clear that French education was not going to be “lockstep” any longer. It was going to be child-centered.

claims.text: The 1989 law granted students the right to elaborate their own curricula according to their own aspirations and capacities.
claims.source_passage: Pupils and students will elaborate their own curricula according to their own aspirations and capacities.

claims.text: The 1989 legislation decentralized curriculum production, requiring each primary school to produce its own curriculum.
claims.source_passage: Thenceforth each primary school was to produce its own curriculum.

claims.text: French teachers trained in the 1960s were indoctrinated in 'la nouvelle education' (progressive education) at their training institutes.
claims.source_passage: They had been instructed, during their training at the écoles normales, in a new point of view—that of la nouvelle education, which we call “progressive education.”

claims.text: Progressive education in France encouraged naturalistic teaching of math and reading while deemphasizing memorization and grammar.
claims.source_passage: It encouraged the naturalistic teaching of math and reading. It deemphasized memorization and grammar.

claims.text: The rise in French primary students failing to read and reckon in the 1970s and 80s was a consequence of new progressive methods, not the old rigorous curriculum.
claims.source_passage: It had not been understood that the rise in the number of students who were unable to read and reckon was a consequence of the new methods, not the old ones!

claims.text: Starting in the 1960s, naturalism, individualism, and skill-centrism began to replace traditional teaching methods in France 'under the radar.'
claims.source_passage: For, starting in the 1960s, under the radar, traditional teaching methods of reading and arithmetic began to give way to the three disastrous pedagogical theories identified in this book: naturalism, individualism, and skill-centrism.

claims.text: Naturalistic reading theories, such as 'la méthode globale,' caused basic reading difficulties in French pupils even before the national curriculum was officially changed.
claims.source_passage: The new naturalistic theories into which French teachers were being indoctrinated—for example, in la méthode globale, and la méthode mixte in reading—had begun to cause basic reading difficulties among the youngest pupils, even as the schools kept a common subject-matter curriculum.

claims.text: The failures caused by progressive methods were paradoxically used as evidence to argue for more progressive reforms and against the national curriculum.
claims.source_passage: The rise in grade retentions, caused by the new methods, was then deployed successfully as a further argument against the old pedagogy and the old uniformity of the topics in the national curriculum.

claims.text: Pierre Bourdieu argued that French republican education was a device for reproducing existing social stratifications rather than overcoming them.
claims.source_passage: They concluded that French republican education was a device for reproducing existing social stratifications. The French school system was unwittingly part of a conspiracy to keep the underclasses submerged. His radical claim: French schooling was a means of reproducing social class, not overcoming it.

claims.text: Bourdieu's analysis of university majors and cultural tastes showed statistically significant correlations with social class.
claims.source_passage: By a clear statistical margin these decisions about a major study area were class based. Bourdieu also analyzed the different tastes in music and movies of university students, and found statistically significant correlations with class.

claims.text: Bourdieu's book 'The Inheritors' is superficial and exaggerates minor statistical disparities into significant societal problems.
claims.source_passage: To be an American reading The Inheritors is to be astonished at its superficiality, its indignant making of mountains out of molehills.

claims.text: Bourdieu's analysis of social reproduction in 'The Inheritors' is superficial because it only accounts for a single generation's time slice.
claims.source_passage: The book embraces far too narrow a time slice in describing the social-class-based preferences of university students—merely a single generation.

claims.text: The French republican school of the 1950s and 1960s effectively enabled social mobility by bringing children of peasants into the university system.
claims.source_passage: What The Inheritors showed unwittingly was that the French republican school of the 1950s and 1960s had been doing an excellent job of bringing children of peasants to the university, thus enabling Bourdieu to study their attitudes.

claims.text: The old French centralized system with a common curriculum was a product of the egalitarian left, designed to ensure equal opportunity.
claims.source_passage: The old French centralized system with a common curriculum was not, as many on the French left imagined, a product of hidebound right-wing politics, but of the old egalitarian left, starting with Condorcet’s plan of 1790, A Common Education for Children, and Napoleon’s meritocratic principle of 'a career open to talents.'

claims.text: The Bourdieu-Gros Report of 1989 was strikingly vague regarding specific curriculum content.
claims.source_passage: Yet to read the Bourdieu-Gros Report, which became the basis for new 1989 law, is to be struck by its vagueness regarding the curriculum.

claims.text: The growth of knowledge does not render broad, 'encyclopedic' factual knowledge vain or obsolete.
claims.source_passage: It stated: 'The growth of knowledge renders vain the ambition of encyclopedism.'... That statement, assumed to be self-evident, is not backed up by any footnotes. Its assumptions are incorrect.

claims.text: Critical-thinking skills and logical reasoning are not general competences that can be acquired independently of specific domain knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Students are to be taught 'elementary logic,' and, by the acquisition of habits of thought, the techniques and cognitive tools which are indispensable in conducting reasoning that is rigorous and thoughtful—those same general competences are required in the reading of texts.' That statement... is not backed up by any footnotes. Its assumptions are incorrect.

claims.text: The 1989 French educational reform recommended an American-style education focused on general skills and student individuality.
claims.source_passage: In essence the Bourdieu-Gros Report recommended American-style education, and that is what the new law went on to require. Its primary principle was that the goal of education to develop general skills and the 'personality' (i.e., the individuality) of the student.

claims.text: The French educational 'debacle' was caused by the same theories—naturalism, individuality, and skill-centrism—that impacted the United States.
claims.source_passage: Thus the intellectual origins of the French 'debacle' were largely the same theories as the ones that created 'a nation at risk' in the United States: the theory that schooling should be natural, and that the aim of schooling should be the development of individuality and critical-thinking skills.

claims.text: The rise in grade repetitions in France was caused by the shift to naturalistic reading and math instruction, rather than the traditional curriculum.
claims.source_passage: Only years later did it become clear that the alarming rise in grade repetitions had not been caused by the old education at all, but was a result of the new naturalistic approach to reading and math.

claims.text: Naturalistic education methods in France intensified social stratification and inequality instead of reducing them.
claims.source_passage: The new methods had not reduced social stratification and inequality but instead had intensified them.

claims.text: The belief that older 'lockstep' education in France was unfair and promoted social stratification was used to justify reform.
claims.source_passage: Older forms of “lockstep” education were not only outmoded but also unfair. They were said to promote continued social stratification.

claims.text: The French Ministry of Education's periodic 'soundings' provide a precise record of how educational policies affect different socio-economic groups.
claims.source_passage: The French Ministry of Education takes periodic “soundings” of the public school system, recording in detail the level of student competencies, along with demographic information about the students.

claims.text: The French data from 1987 to 2007 constitute the most definitive comparative study of curriculum effects ever undertaken in any country.
claims.source_passage: The results are decisive, and constitute the most definitive comparative study of curriculum effects ever undertaken in any country.

claims.text: The adoption of a child-centered skills curriculum caused average French literacy scores to drop by 0.4 standard deviations over twenty years.
claims.source_passage: The child-centered skills curriculum had caused the average of literacy scores to fall four-tenths of a standard deviation.

claims.text: The educational decline in France was less severe for advantaged students because they acquire academic knowledge in the home.
claims.source_passage: Because children with better-educated parents acquire academic knowledge both at home and at school, the decline was less steep among more-advantaged students.

claims.text: The severity of the score decline among needy students in France proves the positive equity effects of a common grade-by-grade curriculum.
claims.source_passage: Among the neediest students, the decline was severe. This differential effect is important. It suggests the positive equity effects of a common grade-by-grade curriculum in the elementary school.

claims.text: Literacy test scores are a direct measure of whether a student has gained the knowledge and vocabulary of the public sphere.
claims.source_passage: If students gain the knowledge and vocabulary of the public sphere, they will score well on a literacy test.

claims.text: Schools must supply enabling knowledge to students who do not acquire it from their home environments to ensure equitable literacy outcomes.
claims.source_passage: If accidents of birth have excluded that knowledge from the home environment, and if the school does not supply it, then they will score badly on a literacy test.

claims.text: The 1990 French educational reforms systematically deprived poor children of the enabling knowledge that wealthy children acquired at home.
claims.source_passage: They systematically deprived poor children of the enabling knowledge that rich children had acquired from their home environments.

claims.text: A specific, cumulative common-core curriculum is superior to an individualized curriculum for achieving both high performance and fairness.
claims.source_passage: The French experiment... has pretty well proved that a specific, cumulative common-core curriculum... is far superior to the American-style individualized curriculum.

claims.text: The 1990 French educational arrangements intensified social stratification instead of diminishing it.
claims.source_passage: But the educational results went the opposite way, intensifying inequalities. As the French data from 1987 to 2007 show, the new 1990 arrangements greatly intensified the social reproduction they were supposed to diminish.

claims.text: The 20-year French study of curricular effects provides more significant data for American education than any data collected within the United States.
claims.source_passage: From a scientific standpoint, the data from France are more significant for American education than any data we have ever managed to collect from our own system.

claims.text: Teacher quality remained constant in France by all objective measures during the period of educational decline.
claims.source_passage: “Teacher quality” remained constant in France by all objective measures during the period of decline.

claims.text: The United States lacks large-scale, curriculum-related data similar to the French data.
claims.source_passage: These findings are important for us because we lack large-scale, curriculum-related data in the United States.

claims.text: Any US district implementing a communal, cumulative curriculum in all subjects will show higher achievement and equity than one under the skills-based paradigm.
claims.source_passage: These data make it nearly certain that any district in the United States that implemented a communal, cumulative curriculum in all subjects (not just language arts and math) will show higher achievement and much higher equity than it has shown in the past under the reign of the skills delusion.

claims.text: The results of the French shift to individualized curricula are clear enough that continuing such a program would be considered unethical in medical research.
claims.source_passage: If these trials had occurred in medical research, with epidemiological data from several thousand randomly assigned experimental subjects, they would quickly be halted. It would be considered unethical to continue them.

claims.text: French education schools required teachers to act as 'resource persons' or 'guides on the side' rather than instructors.
claims.source_passage: Education colleges required a teacher to be called a “resource person,” a guide on the side.

claims.text: The chief aim of the new French pedagogy was the imparting of all-purpose skills like 'learning to learn' rather than information.
claims.source_passage: The chief aim was the imparting of all-purpose skills like “learning to learn.”

claims.text: French teacher-training institutes mandated naturalistic 'whole-language' or 'balanced literacy' approaches over phonics.
claims.source_passage: Rachel Boutonnet was told that students must cease to be taught by phonics. They should learn to read by the naturalistic “whole-language” approach, or... “balanced literacy.”

claims.text: French education schools were dominated by progressive methods as early as 1977.
claims.source_passage: From his book’s opening pages he made clear that the methods and attitudes Boutonnet described were already dominant in education schools more than twenty years before.

claims.text: Progressive, 'modern' educational methods like 'natural' reading and self-learning were already dominant in French teacher-training schools by 1977.
claims.source_passage: From his book’s opening pages he made clear that the methods and attitudes Boutonnet described were already dominant in education schools more than twenty years before. He began his book as follows: For twenty years, the National Education system has prevented me from doing my job. When I left the normal school in 1977, I was a young progressive teacher, convinced of the superiority of the reading method called 'natural.'

claims.text: Students taught using traditional methods like phonics and dictation outperformed those taught with modern, child-centered methods.
claims.source_passage: Yet students of older teachers, who dared to continue to use dictations and learn reading by phonics, fared better. My students, pampered by modern methods, have suffered educational disadvantages of which I am ashamed today.

claims.text: Modern education serves to justify the abandonment of high academic ambitions for children.
claims.source_passage: Modern education only serves to justify the abandonment of ambitions that we have for our children. We have before us a real cultural catastrophe.

claims.text: The disintegration of the primary school curriculum is the primary cause of the French educational crisis.
claims.source_passage: We situate the main origin of the school crisis in the disintegration of the curriculum—principally the primary school curriculum.

claims.text: A disintegrated curriculum makes taught subject matter random, which prevents students from gaining the wider knowledge necessary for academic success.
claims.source_passage: this curricular disintegration has rendered more or less random the subject matters taught. It has thus compromised the possibility for the majority of students to gain the wider knowledge that is necessary to academic success.

claims.text: The reconstruction of a rich, coherent, and cumulative primary curriculum is the necessary condition for the true democratization of education.
claims.source_passage: In consequence, we see the reconstruction of the primary curriculum into one that is rich, coherent, and cumulative as the necessary condition for a true democratization of education.

claims.text: By 2012, France had deteriorated more in educational equity than any other country in the world.
claims.source_passage: the most recent of which, in 2012, showed that France, of all countries in the world, has deteriorated the most in the equity of its schools.

claims.text: The 2005 law introducing a common 'socle' (base) of knowledge failed because the resulting standards were too vague and lacked specifics.
claims.source_passage: A new law in 2005 reconstituted the idea that there should be a more significant base, or socle, that all children should learn, but the resulting document did not get into specifics, and has been essentially useless.

claims.text: The French educational system has become the most inegalitarian in Europe due to its condemnation of the 'transmission' of culture.
claims.source_passage: Fifty years after The Inheritors, our educational system, greatly influenced by that book’s condemnation of transmission, has become the most inegalitarian in Europe.

claims.text: The failure of public education forces parents to use tactical maneuvers (like choosing specific neighborhoods or subjects) to ensure their children's success.
claims.source_passage: The only survivors of the shipwreck of our public education are those whose parents know the tactics for escaping the disaster—which quartiers to live in, which subjects to emphasize, which options to choose.

claims.text: Renouncing the teaching of national culture in the name of avoiding social selection has specifically disadvantaged children of immigrant families.
claims.source_passage: On the other end of the spectrum are the designated victims of social selection—among them, the children of immigrant families. It is with them in mind that we renounced teaching the culture of our country and transmitting to all a common heritage.

claims.text: The denial of cultural transmission in schools will eventually lead to social instability and violence.
claims.source_passage: Who can fail to see that that is the true violence; that such denial of transmission cannot fail, one day, to explode?

claims.text: Pierre Bourdieu, despite his humble origins, would have almost no chance of entering the elite École Normale Supérieure in the current French educational system.
claims.source_passage: Pierre Bourdieu, son of an agricultural worker in a little village of Béarn: would he today have the slightest chance of entering the école normale supérieure? Without any doubt, no.

claims.text: French public education has become a 'shipwreck' where only children with parents savvy enough to navigate specific neighborhoods and subjects can succeed.
claims.source_passage: The only survivors of the shipwreck of our public education are those whose parents know the tactics for escaping the disaster—which quartiers to live in, which subjects to emphasize, which options to choose.

claims.text: The decision to stop teaching a common national heritage and culture specifically targeted and harmed immigrant families.
claims.source_passage: On the other end of the spectrum are the designated victims of social selection—among them, the children of immigrant families. It is with them in mind that we renounced teaching the culture of our country and transmitting to all a common heritage.

claims.text: Children of immigrant families in France now face double the average risk of serious academic failure due to the denial of cultural transmission.
claims.source_passage: Today, those young people have twice the average risk of serious academic failure.

claims.text: The delusion regarding decentralization, individualism, and general skills is shared by both the political left and the political right.
claims.source_passage: Bourdieu and the political left cannot boast sole ownership of decentralization, individualism, and the skills delusion. Sweden now offers a similarly compelling example from the right.

claims.text: Sweden's 1994 educational reforms combined the 'providential' impulses of the individual child with free-market principles.
claims.source_passage: Sweden decided in 1994 to make the same sort of educational changes as France. Behind this move lay the same kind of thinking about following the providential impulses of the individual child, to which Sweden added the further idea of following the providential workings of the free market.

claims.text: Decentralization in Sweden caused a severe decline in student abilities in core subjects like reading, writing, and arithmetic.
claims.source_passage: He showed that decentralization had caused a severe drop in students’ abilities in reading, writing, and arithmetic.

claims.text: The decentralization of the Swedish curriculum greatly widened achievement gaps between wealthy and poor students.
claims.source_passage: It had greatly widened the achievement gaps between rich and poor students.

claims.text: Before 1994, the Swedish school system was one of the most egalitarian in the world, with minimal performance differences based on student wealth or school location.
claims.source_passage: Before the changes occurred, Lewin showed, Swedish schools exhibited only minor gaps between rich and poor students, and Sweden boasted one of the most egalitarian systems in the world.

claims.text: The 1994 Swedish reforms replaced specific content requirements with vague time allocations and local determination of knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Under the new curricula, the state largely abandoned the ambition to determine the knowledge content of the school, and transferred that determination to local schools.

claims.text: The belief that local teachers and principals are better positioned to design curricula because of 'local conditions' is a foundational but flawed idea of the 1994 Swedish law.
claims.source_passage: In contrast, the basic idea of 1994 law was that henceforth teachers and principals are considered to be in the best position to design the curriculum, because they best know the local conditions.

claims.text: Sweden experienced the largest drop in verbal scores ever recorded by PISA between 2000 and 2012.
claims.source_passage: It is the largest drop ever recorded by PISA.

claims.text: The magnitude of Sweden's educational decline makes it as significant a warning for American policy as the French decline.
claims.source_passage: The magnitude of the Swedish decline makes the change from a common-core curriculum throughout Sweden to an individualistic approach almost as significant for American policy as the French decline

claims.text: High equity and achievement in Sweden prior to 1994 were the intentional results of a national policy focused on a common curriculum.
claims.source_passage: Before 1994, high equity and achievement in Sweden had been no accident. It had been a chief aim of national policy.

claims.text: Extra resources allocated to low-achieving students are most effective when paired with 'equality-oriented centralism.'
claims.source_passage: Sweden followed what he calls “equality-oriented centralism,” with extra resources allocated to low achieving students. Under the Swedish system, as it evolved, “the differences in achievement between different schools were rather small.”

claims.text: The legislative principle of 'equivalence'—adapting education to individual needs—actually replaced and undermined the previous success of the common curriculum.
claims.source_passage: A principle of “equivalence” was legislated to replace the common curriculum... Education should be adapted to each pupil’s circumstances and needs, based on the pupils’ background, earlier experiences, language, and knowledge

claims.text: Before 1994, Sweden followed a model of 'equality-oriented centralism' that resulted in very small differences in achievement between different schools.
claims.source_passage: According to Professor Tomas Englund of Örebro University, Sweden followed what he calls “equality-oriented centralism,” with extra resources allocated to low achieving students. Under the Swedish system, as it evolved, “the differences in achievement between different schools were rather small.”

claims.text: The Swedish law of 1994 discarded the common national curriculum and replaced it with a principle of 'equivalence.'
claims.source_passage: The new law of 1994 discarded those arrangements. A principle of “equivalence” was legislated to replace the common curriculum

claims.text: Under the principle of 'equivalence,' Swedish education was no longer required to be the same across different locations.
claims.source_passage: The Education Act stipulates that the education provided within each type of school should be of equivalent value, irrespective of where in the country it is provided... However, equivalent education does not mean that the education should be the same everywhere.

claims.text: The Swedish reform of 1994 substituted specific subject content with a focus on generic 'thinking skills' such as critical thinking and problem solving.
claims.source_passage: All pupils will be taught different, individually adapted topics—but all will be taught the same thinking skills: “Students should train themselves to think critically, to examine the facts and circumstances and to realize the consequences of different options.Pupils shall develop their ability to take initiative and responsibility and to work and solve problems both independently and with others."

claims.text: The emphasis on individuality and thinking skills caused the Swedish primary curriculum to become incoherent.
claims.source_passage: With the new emphasis on individuality and thinking skills, the elementary curriculum in Sweden became as incoherent as the curriculum had become in France and the United States.

claims.text: France and Sweden undermined their effective school systems because they were misled by slogans regarding individualization and skills.
claims.source_passage: Why did France and Sweden, which stood at the top in equity and achievement, suddenly destroy two of the most admirable and effective large school systems in the world? It was because they intended to make their good systems even better. They were unlucky enough to believe the well-meant but misguided slogans about individualization and skills.

claims.text: According to PISA studies, Sweden is the second-worst country, after France, in terms of the deterioration of social equality in education.
claims.source_passage: Now in the PISA studies Sweden is second only to France as the country that has deteriorated the most in social equality.

claims.text: Germany improved its PISA standings in both achievement and equity by adopting well-defined, grade-by-grade curricula.
claims.source_passage: Germany is an object lesson in the other direction. It has greatly improved its PISA standings in both achievement and equity. ... Reacting to the Schock, Germany began to adopt well-defined curricula. After that it began to score above average for developed nations.

claims.text: Curriculum specificity improves student outcomes because it clarifies exactly what teachers must teach and what students must learn.
claims.source_passage: “The teachers,” he said, “know specifically what to teach, and the students know what they have to learn.”

claims.text: The shift toward individualization and skills-based education is not an exclusively left-wing or right-wing ideological project.
claims.source_passage: Sweden offers dramatic proof that left-versus-right ideological simplifications regarding the declines there and in France are not warranted. In Sweden, the disastrous movement toward individualism and skills that destroyed its education came from the political right, not the left as in France.

claims.text: The Swedish political right's justification for individualization was that schools would perform better without state interference in curriculum commonality.
claims.source_passage: The theory offered in Sweden was that individual schools and students, left on their own without state interference, would yield better results than state-imposed commonality.

claims.text: The 1967 Plowden Report encouraged British schools to adopt progressivism, leading to disastrous results.
claims.source_passage: In 1967, before the Bourdieu-Gros Report of 1989, Britain had issued the Plowden Report under the auspices of a left-leaning Labor government. Progressive education, long an undercurrent in teacher training in the United Kingdom, now beg[an]

claims.text: The educational reform in Sweden was based on the theory that individual schools and students, left without state interference, would achieve better results than through state-imposed commonality.
claims.source_passage: The theory offered in Sweden was that individual schools and students, left on their own without state interference, would yield better results than state-imposed commonality.

claims.text: The educational position of the political right in Sweden regarding individualized education is nearly identical to that of the socialist left in France.
claims.source_passage: That statement from the right was exactly the position and almost the wording of the socialist left in France, which stated: 'The school shall take into account the plurality and diversity of aptitudes of each pupil.'

claims.text: The 1967 Plowden Report institutionalized progressive education as official left-wing doctrine in Great Britain.
claims.source_passage: Progressive education, long an undercurrent in teacher training in the United Kingdom, now began to be institutionalized as official left-wing doctrine.

claims.text: Individualized education methods in Britain exacerbated social inequalities without leading to improvements in student creativity.
claims.source_passage: The Bennett Report of 1976 argued that the data showed that the new methods were exacerbating inequalities without leading to any improvement in 'creativity.'

claims.text: Teacher unions' advocacy for 'personalization' and 'individuality' is a result of indoctrination within progressive teacher-training institutions.
claims.source_passage: Their membership has simply been indoctrinated by their teacher-training institutions, which are theological institutes for progressivism.

claims.text: The American Federation of Teachers (AFT) supports the Core Knowledge Curriculum because it narrows achievement gaps and provides social empowerment.
claims.source_passage: In the United States, the independent-minded American Federation of Teachers has long supported the highly specific Core Knowledge Curriculum because of its socially enabling power, and its effectiveness in narrowing achievement gaps between groups.

claims.text: Historically, the political left (including Condorcet and Jefferson) favored universalism—offering the same educational opportunities to all children.
claims.source_passage: Ever since Condorcet and Jefferson, the left has favored universalism—that is, the idea of offering each child the very same educational opportunities in the early years.

claims.text: Educational individualism and the 'skills delusion' have no inherent or necessary connection to either the political left or the political right.
claims.source_passage: Educational individualism and the skills delusion have no inherent connection with either the left or right.

claims.text: Progressive education is fundamentally weak because it lacks a coherent theory of social welfare, tending instead toward anarchy or extreme individualism.
claims.source_passage: The great weakness of Progressive Education lies in the fact that it has elaborated no theory of social welfare, unless it be that of anarchy or extreme individualism.

claims.text: Poor children need to master the established intellectual 'tools of power' rather than being encouraged to invent their own knowledge.
claims.source_passage: With his unerring penetration, he understood that poor children needed to master the intellectual tools of power rather than implausibly trying to invent their own.

claims.text: Broadly shared communal knowledge is a cognitive prerequisite for the effective use of language.
claims.source_passage: cognitive science clearly indicates that broad, commonly shared knowledge is essential for effective language use.

claims.text: Antonio Gramsci advocated for a 'unitary school' of general culture to provide humanistic formation for all children.
claims.source_passage: The phrase 'la scuola unitaria o di formazione umanistica . . . o di cultura generale' [the unitary school or the school of humanistic formation or the school of general culture] is interesting because it explains that the 'common school' Gramsci was thinking of was 'a school of humanistic formation.'

claims.text: Broad, commonly shared knowledge is essential for effective language use according to cognitive science.
claims.source_passage: cognitive science clearly indicates that broad, commonly shared knowledge is essential for effective language use.

claims.text: Antonio Gramsci supported a common curriculum for all young children for reasons of equal opportunity.
claims.source_passage: Gramsci the 'common school' is necessary for 'reasons of equal opportunity,' as you put it, and to remedy the tendency of modernity and modernization to channel pupils/students into special(ized) schools meant to prepare them for technical, industrial, etc. jobs.

claims.text: The 'common school' concept was a foundational element in the construction of American democracy.
claims.source_passage: The 'common school' is, of course, the very phrase and concept that helped build American democracy.

claims.text: The content of early education should not be left to the 'nature of the child' or the 'market.'
claims.source_passage: repuadiating misplaced providential ideas that leave the content of early education to benign forces in the nature of the child—or of the market.

claims.text: The early education curriculum for grades preK–5 requires hard, specific thought and commonality.
claims.source_passage: The early curriculum requires hard, specific thought, and commonality in grades preK–5.

claims.text: The most important practical theme of 'Why Knowledge Matters' is the depoliticizing of primary education.
claims.source_passage: The depoliticizing of primary education could be the most important practical theme of this book.

claims.text: Individualism in the early grades disables individuals rather than empowering them.
claims.source_passage: Individualism in the early grades disables many individuals.

claims.text: Providential individualism is a primary cause of the failure to narrow achievement gaps in the United States, Britain, France, and Sweden.
claims.source_passage: Individualism with its curricular incoherence has been a chief cause of stasis or worse in the effort to narrow the achievement gaps between racial, ethnic, and economic groups in the United States, Britain, France, and Sweden.

claims.text: Providential individualism is an outmoded intellectual residue rather than a logical necessity for any political party.
claims.source_passage: Providential individualism is an outmoded residue, a sedimentary deposit from intellectual history. It is not a logical nor a necessary element in the aims of either left or right.

claims.text: An effective individualized curriculum in the early grades is an impossible dream if equality of opportunity is the goal.
claims.source_passage: If equality of opportunity remains a primary aim, both parties will need to disavow the impossible dream of an effective individualized curriculum in the early grades.

claims.text: Differentiated curricula based on tracking is negatively associated with student performance on international assessments.
claims.source_passage: Use of a differentiated curriculum based on tracking is negatively associated with student performance on the international assessments and also reduces opportunities for some students to be exposed to more advanced curriculum.

claims.text: The educational decline in France following 1989 resulted from mistakes that were entirely avoidable, mirroring the avoidable military mistakes of 1940.
claims.source_passage: His view, which I agree with, is that top French intellectuals made big avoidable mistakes in 1989, just as higher-ups had made serious, avoidable military mistakes in 1940.

claims.text: By 1936, progressive public schools were already numbered in the thousands in the United States, indicating the early roots of the shift from content-based education.
claims.source_passage: Already, by 1936, there were thousands of progressive public schools in the United States according to a March of Time newsreel of that year entitled “New Schools for Old.”

claims.text: The verbal score decline in the US during 1965–1975 was broad-based, affecting the SAT (1/3 SD), ACT (1/4 SD), and state tests in Iowa and Minnesota (1/3 and 1/4 SD respectively).
claims.source_passage: The SAT dropped by one-third in that first decade... the ACT—a precollege test similar to the SAT—dropped by one-quarter. The Iowa Test of Educational Development... dropped by almost one-third; the Minnesota College Aptitude Test... dropped by one-quarter.

claims.text: Pedagogical knowledge and educational cures should be imported across national boundaries in the same manner as medical and scientific discoveries.
claims.source_passage: Yet if there were some persistent illness in New York City whose cause and cure had been determined by researchers in Paris, we wouldn’t hesitate to import that scientific knowledge to cure New York... We should not hesitate to import that knowledge.

claims.text: France has a diverse population that is demographically comparable in size to the combined populations of California and Texas.
claims.source_passage: France has a diverse population as big as that of California and Texas combined. We need to take note.

claims.text: In the 1980s, France was more successful at educating immigrants than any other nation in Europe.
claims.source_passage: France exhibited the highest average student achievement with the greatest equity of any large, diverse country. It educated immigrants better than any other European nation.

claims.text: The 1989 French educational reform law passed by a narrow margin in the National Assembly (280 votes for to 266 against), showing significant opposition even at inception.
claims.source_passage: In 1989, the Left in the National Assembly (Socialists plus Communists) had an absolute majority; they could pass any law they wished. The vote was 280 in favor, 266 against.

claims.text: Bourdieu's 'The Inheritors' is flawed because it draws conclusions about social reproduction based on a single generation of data rather than observing shifts over multiple generations.
claims.source_passage: The book embraces far too narrow a time slice in describing the social-class-based preferences of university students—merely a single generation... To imagine just one generation ahead makes the book’s astonishing indignation... seem a head-shaking example of the mood of the 1960s.

claims.text: Rachel Boutonnet's unfavorable evaluation despite her students' high performance demonstrates how ideological conformity in education takes precedence over empirical results.
claims.source_passage: After graduation, she secretly taught her students to read via the phonics method, and they performed above the norm... But, no, she received an unfavorable report. She had not followed methods of 'whole language'...

claims.text: Germany's successful PISA recovery was driven by its 'Länder' (states) creating curricula that were more specifically defined and better aligned with each other.
claims.source_passage: Manfred Prenzel... had a simple answer: the post-Schock curriculum guides put out by the Länder... are now much more aligned with each other, and are more specific grade by grade.

claims.text: The depoliticizing of primary education should be the central practical objective for nations seeking to improve school quality.
claims.source_passage: The depoliticizing of primary education could be the most important practical theme of this book.

claims.text: If equality of opportunity is to be achieved, both the political left and right must abandon the goal of individualized curricula in the early grades.
claims.source_passage: If equality of opportunity remains a primary aim, both parties will need to disavow the impossible dream of an effective individualized curriculum in the early grades.

evidence.description: 2007 French Ministry of Education data compiled by Paola Mattei showing a decline in achievement and equity in France.
evidence.source_passage: I first decided to write this book after I ran across the astonishing 2007 data compiled by the French Ministry of Education, recently placed on the Web by Paola Mattei of the University of Oxford.

evidence.description: SAT verbal score drop of one-half standard deviation between 1960 and 1980.
evidence.source_passage: SAT verbal scores had plummeted half a standard deviation in the twenty years between 1960 and 1980.

evidence.description: Comparative score drops (1965-1975) across the ACT, Iowa Test of Educational Development, Minnesota College Aptitude Test, and Stanford Achievement Test.
evidence.source_reference: Robert Franciosi, The Rise and Fall of American Public Schools (2004)
evidence.source_passage: In that same decade, the ACT—a precollege test similar to the SAT—dropped by one-quarter. The Iowa Test of Educational Development, given to all Iowa juniors and seniors, dropped by almost one-third; the Minnesota College Aptitude Test, given to all seniors, dropped by one-quarter.

evidence.description: Historical newsreel footage documenting the spread of progressive schools in the US by 1936.
evidence.source_reference: March of Time, 'New Schools for Old' (1936)
evidence.source_passage: Already, by 1936, there were thousands of progressive public schools in the United States according to a March of Time newsreel of that year entitled “New Schools for Old.”

evidence.description: State tests in Iowa and Minnesota during periods of stable demographics.
evidence.source_reference: L. A. Munday, Declining Admissions Test Scores (1976)
evidence.source_passage: Particularly persuasive are the state tests in Iowa and Minnesota, administered to virtually all high school juniors and seniors in those states at a time when their pupil demographics were stable—thus eliminating demographic change as a cause of the declines in those states.

evidence.description: Stable demographic data in Iowa and Minnesota showing that score declines occurred independently of changes in the student population.
Figure 7.1 showing 1991 Literacy levels for ninth graders, placing France and Sweden at the top for both advantaged and disadvantaged students.
The French national curriculum document (pre-1989), a 100-page paperback allocating specific minutes and topics to every grade.
French preschool attendance data showing nearly 100% participation for 4 and 5-year-olds in the 1980s.
Policy statement by the French President emphasizing common culture as the key to social unity and justice.
The foundational document for the 1989 reforms that advocated for pluralism and criticized 'encyclopedism' and 'the piling up of knowledge.'
The specific legislative act that decentralized the French school system and mandated child-centered pedagogy.
The 1989 Bourdieu-Gros Report which provided the intellectual basis for the 1989 law.
The preamble and text of the 1989 French education law (loi Jospin).
The 1964 book 'The Inheritors' by Bourdieu and Passeron.
Statistics regarding grade repetition and failure in reading and arithmetic in French primary schools.
The adoption of 'la méthode globale' and 'la méthode mixte' in French classrooms starting in the 1960s.
Bourdieu's statistical correlation between university majors and student class background in 1960s France.
The Bourdieu-Gros Report's explicit recommendation to focus on 'elementary logic' and 'general competences' over factual knowledge.
evidence.source_passage: Students are to be taught 'elementary logic,' and, by the acquisition of habits of thought, the techniques and cognitive tools which are indispensable in conducting reasoning that is rigorous and thoughtful

evidence.description: French Ministry of Education periodic 'soundings' (surveys) recording student level of competencies and demographics.
French Ministry of Education 'soundings' from 1987, 1997, and 2007 using identical test instruments to measure 10-year-olds.
evidence.source_passage: In 1987, the French Ministry had taken one of its periodic national soundings of student achievement at the end of fifth grade... A second sounding, using the same test instruments, was conducted in 1997. Then another... in 2007.

evidence.description: Literacy scores in France declined by four-tenths of a standard deviation (z-score from 0 to -0.37) between 1987 and 2007.
evidence.source_passage: The child-centered skills curriculum had caused the average of literacy scores to fall four-tenths of a standard deviation.

evidence.description: Differential decline in scores between demographic groups, showing children of unemployed/laborers fell much further than children of professionals/intellectuals.
evidence.source_passage: Among the neediest students, the decline was severe. This differential effect is important.

evidence.description: The 1989 Bourdieu-Gros Report which mandated a revolution in the French educational system toward individualism.
evidence.source_passage: The Bourdieu-Gros Report demanded a total overhaul of the system to make it more egalitarian, and demanded nothing less than a revolution of the system.

evidence.description: French data from 1987 to 2007 showing that new school arrangements intensified social reproduction.
evidence.source_passage: As the French data from 1987 to 2007 show, the new 1990 arrangements greatly intensified the social reproduction they were supposed to diminish.

evidence.description: The maintainance of high entry standards for French teachers before and after the 1989 reforms.
evidence.source_passage: I would stress that entry standards for teachers into the French school system remained extremely high both before and after Jospin.

evidence.description: Rachel Boutonnet's first-hand account of ideological conformity and 'learning-to-learn' mandates in French teacher training.
evidence.source_reference: Rachel Boutonnet, Journal d’une Institutrice Clandestine (2003)
evidence.source_passage: She was attending one of the new education schools in order to get her license but was so outraged by the boredom, misinformation, and ideological conformity thrust upon her that she resolved to keep a journal.

evidence.description: Marc Le Bris's professional experience as a teacher who initially followed progressive methods before seeing their failure.
evidence.source_reference: Marc Le Bris, Et Vos Enfants ne Sauront pas Lire (2004)
evidence.source_passage: When I left the normal school in 1977, I was a young progressive teacher, convinced of the superiority of the reading method called “natural.” I believed everything.

evidence.description: Marc Le Bris's book 'Your Children Won’t Know How to Read or Count' detailing his experience with 'natural' reading methods and their failure.
evidence.source_reference: Marc Le Bris, Et Vos Enfants ne Sauront pas Lire . . . Ni Compter (2004)

Analysis by GRIP (Group for Interdisciplinary Studies of the Curriculum) on the disintegration of the primary curriculum.
François-Xavier Bellamy's book 'The Disinherited' which deconstructs current French educational ideas and their outcomes.
The 2014 Lewin Report commissioned by the Swedish government which analyzed the effects of school decentralization.

The Swedish Education Act of 1994, which codified the principle of individualized adaptation over a common curriculum.

The testimony of Manfred Prenzel regarding the cause of German educational improvement.

evidence.source_reference: Swedish study cited in The Economist (2014)

evidence.description: Swedish government policy statement on adapting education to individual circumstances and backgrounds.
The Plowden Report (1967) which advocated for child-centered, discovery-based learning in British schools.
The Bennett Report (1976) which showed that progressive methods in Britain increased inequality without raising creativity.
evidence.source_reference: Neville Bennett, Teaching Styles and Pupil Progress (1976)

George Counts' 1932 address to the Progressive Education Association critiquing the movement's lack of social theory.
evidence.source_reference: George Counts, 'Dare Progressive Education Be Progressive?' (1932)

Cross-national observation of stasis in achievement gaps in the US, Britain, France, and Sweden under individualistic educational models.
Medrich (1992) report on international mathematics and science assessment findings regarding tracking and performance.

evidence.source_reference: Gabriel H. Sahlgren, Schooling for Money, IEA Discussion Paper 33 (2010)
